text: The surgeon uses the dominant right hand to grasp the needle one-third of the distance from the swedge to the tip, ensuring proper orientation for tissue penetration. The needle enters the tissue at a sixty to seventy-five degree angle and is rotated through the tissue following its curve. Axial needle drivers, such as Ethicon German needle drivers, are used to facilitate this step. The non-dominant hand stabilizes the tissue and grasps the needle tip as it emerges, locking the grasper to secure the needle.

The surgeon completes the laparoscopic knot tying process by positioning the needle at a 45-degree angle and performing a series of wraps. The needle is kept north of the knot, ensuring proper orientation of the suture material. The dominant hand wraps the suture with two clockwise turns, followed by a counterclockwise rotation, and finishes with a single clockwise wrap, securing the knot.
